section.title: Firstfruits and Tithes
section.sectionRationale: This chapter closes the law section with worship: bringing the first and best of the harvest with a recited confession of Israel's whole redemption story, giving the third-year tithe to the vulnerable, and a covenant renewal formula, the LORD as Israel's God, Israel as His treasured people, sealing everything that has come before.
section.thesis: Having given Israel its statutes, the LORD now teaches Israel how to worship in response: bringing firstfruits with a recited history of grace, from a wandering Aramean, to slavery, to redemption, to the land, sharing the tithe with the vulnerable, and confessing on this day that the LORD is Israel's God and Israel is His treasured possession.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the offering of firstfruits with its recited confession of Israel's whole story of grace; the third-year tithe for the Levite, foreigner, fatherless, and widow with its declaration of obedience; and the covenant's mutual, this-day confession, the LORD as Israel's God, Israel as His treasured people.

verses.text: When you enter the land that the LORD your God is giving you as an inheritance, and you take possession of it and settle in it,

you are to take some of the {{bikkurim|firstfruits}} of all your produce from the soil of the land that the LORD your God is giving you and put them in a basket. Then go to the place the LORD your God will choose as a dwelling for His Name,
to the priest who is serving at that time, and say to him, "I declare today to the LORD your God that I have entered the land that the LORD swore to our fathers to give us."
Then the priest shall take the basket from your hands and place it before the altar of the LORD your God,
and you are to declare before the LORD your God, "My father was a {{oved|wandering}} Aramean, and he went down to Egypt few in number and lived there and became a great nation, mighty and numerous.
But the Egyptians mistreated us and afflicted us, putting us to hard labor.
So we called out to the LORD, the God of our fathers; and the LORD heard our voice and saw our affliction, toil, and oppression.
Then the LORD brought us out of Egypt with a mighty hand and an outstretched arm, with great terror, signs, and wonders.
And He brought us to this place and gave us this land, a land flowing with milk and honey.
And now, behold, I have brought the firstfruits of the land that You, O LORD, have given me." Then you are to place the basket before the LORD your God and bow down before Him.
So you shall rejoice, you, the Levite, and the foreigner dwelling among you, in all the good things the LORD your God has given to you and your household.

When you have finished laying aside a tenth of all your produce in the third year, the year of the tithe, you are to give it to the Levite, the foreigner, the fatherless, and the widow, that they may eat and be filled within your gates.

Then you shall declare in the presence of the LORD your God, "I have removed from my house the sacred portion and have given it to the Levite, the foreigner, the fatherless, and the widow, according to all the commandments You have given me. I have not transgressed or forgotten Your commandments.

I have not eaten any of the sacred portion while in mourning, or removed any of it while unclean, or offered any of it for the dead. I have obeyed the LORD my God; I have done everything You commanded me.
Look down from Your holy habitation, from heaven, and bless Your people Israel and the land You have given us as You swore to our fathers, a land flowing with milk and honey."

units.label: A covenant confessed, this day
units.note: the LORD your God, His treasured people

verses.text: The LORD your God commands you this day to follow these statutes and ordinances. You must be careful to follow them with all your heart and with all your soul.
Today you have proclaimed that the LORD is your God and that you will walk in His ways, keep His statutes and commandments and ordinances, and listen to His voice.
And today the LORD has proclaimed that you are His people and {{segullah|treasured possession}} as He promised, that you are to keep all His commandments,
that He will set you high in praise and name and honor above all the nations He has made, and that you will be a holy people to the LORD your God, as He has promised.

oved.gloss: Wandering, perishing, about to be lost. The declaration 'my father was a wandering Aramean' recalls Jacob's precarious, landless beginnings, grounding Israel's worship in the memory of God's rescue of the helpless.

bikkurim.gloss: Firstfruits, the first and best of the harvest, offered in gratitude and trust before the rest of the produce is used or enjoyed.

segullah.gloss: Treasured possession, a king's own private treasure. Used of Israel as God's own particular, cherished people, distinct from His general ownership of all creation, and later applied to the church, 1 Peter 2:9.

basic.context: As the specific law code closes, Moses teaches Israel a liturgy: bringing the first and best of every harvest to the place the LORD chooses, and reciting there the whole story of God's grace, from Jacob's precarious wandering, through slavery in Egypt, to rescue and the gift of the land. Every third year the tithe goes instead to the Levite, foreigner, fatherless, and widow, with its own spoken declaration of obedience. The chapter closes with a mutual confession: the LORD is Israel's God, and Israel is His treasured people.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real, historically specific liturgy for worship in the land God was giving Israel, tied to its own agricultural calendar and its own redemption from Egypt; the deeper pattern, worship as grateful recitation of God's saving acts rather than bare ritual, and generosity toward the vulnerable as an act of obedience, remains for God's people in every era.
basic.originalLanguages: The confession recalls Jacob as an oved, a wandering, nearly perishing, Aramean, deliberately humbling Israel's origin story before recounting the LORD's rescue. The offering itself is bikkurim, firstfruits, the first and best given in trust before the rest is used. And Israel is called God's segullah, treasured possession, a king's own private treasure, not merely one nation among many He happens to own.
basic.typology: The recited confession, 'my father was a wandering Aramean,' models the pattern of gospel proclamation itself: naming helplessness honestly, then recounting God's saving action in history, the very shape of apostolic preaching in Acts.
basic.christ: Israel's identity as God's segullah, treasured possession, is picked up directly in 1 Peter 2:9, where the church, Jew and Gentile united in Christ, is called 'a chosen people... God's own possession,' the pattern set here fulfilled and widened in the people gathered around Jesus.
basic.god: God delights to be recounted, not merely believed in silently: Israel's worship is built around retelling exactly what He has done, and His generosity toward Israel becomes the model for Israel's own generosity toward the Levite, foreigner, fatherless, and widow.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Peter 2:9 applies 'God's own possession' directly to the church. Exodus 19:5 first calls Israel God's treasured possession at Sinai. Titus 2:14 says Christ redeemed a people 'of His own, eager to do good works.' Nehemiah 9 offers a later, similar historical recitation of God's saving acts.
basic.oneStory: Israel's worship rehearses the whole story of grace so far, from wandering exile to promised land, and calls Israel God's own treasured people, a pattern and a title the New Testament takes up to describe the church, gathered from every nation through Christ, as God's treasured possession.

context.literary: This chapter concludes the long section of case law begun in chapter 12 with a return to worship and covenant confession, forming a fitting close before the blessings and curses of chapters 27-28.
context.historical: The firstfruits offering and its recited confession likely formed part of Israel's actual worship practice at the central sanctuary, later the temple, connecting every worshiper personally to the whole sweep of the nation's redemption history.
context.canonical: The title 'treasured possession' first given to Israel at Sinai, Exodus 19:5, and repeated here, becomes one of the New Testament's central images for the church in 1 Peter 2:9, showing continuity between old and new covenant identity.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To give Israel a worship liturgy that roots gratitude in historical memory of God's grace and ties covenant identity to both confession and concrete generosity toward the vulnerable.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specific agricultural and liturgical details, firstfruits at the central sanctuary, the third-year tithe cycle, describe Israel's particular old covenant worship; the underlying pattern, worship as grateful recitation of what God has done, and generosity flowing from that gratitude, carries forward as an enduring pattern for God's people.

debates.question: Does the third-year tithe of verses 12-15 replace or supplement the regular annual tithe described elsewhere in Deuteronomy?

views.label: A distinct, additional welfare tithe every third year
views.case: Many interpreters see this as a separate tithe, beyond the annual tithe of chapter 14, specifically directed to the poor and vulnerable rather than consumed at the central sanctuary, forming a structured, recurring welfare provision.

views.label: The same tithe redirected in its third-year cycle
views.case: Others read this as the same tithe of chapter 14, which in most years was eaten by the family at the sanctuary, being redirected every third year specifically to local storage for the Levite, foreigner, fatherless, and widow.

debates.bearing: Either way, the text establishes a recurring, structured provision for the vulnerable built into Israel's worship calendar, not a matter of occasional charity alone.

study.originalLanguages: The confession recalls Jacob as an oved, wandering, verse 5, deliberately humbling Israel's origin story before recounting the LORD's rescue. The offering itself is bikkurim, firstfruits, verse 2. And Israel is called God's segullah, treasured possession, verse 18, a king's own private treasure.

typology.type: The confession of Israel's story at the firstfruits offering
typology.fulfillment: Models the gospel pattern of proclaiming God's saving acts in history, the shape of apostolic preaching in Acts.

typology.type: Israel as the LORD's treasured possession
typology.fulfillment: Applied directly to the church in Christ, 1 Peter 2:9.

study.christ: Israel's identity as God's segullah, treasured possession, is picked up directly in 1 Peter 2:9, where the church, Jew and Gentile united in Christ, is called 'a chosen people... God's own possession,' the pattern set here fulfilled and widened in the people gathered around Jesus.
study.god: God delights to be recounted, not merely believed in silently: Israel's worship is built around retelling exactly what He has done, and His generosity toward Israel becomes the model for Israel's own generosity toward the Levite, foreigner, fatherless, and widow.

crossReferences.note: Applies 'God's own possession' directly to the church.
First calls Israel God's treasured possession at Sinai.
Christ redeemed a people 'of His own, eager to do good works.'
Offers a later, similar historical recitation of God's saving acts.

study.oneStory: Israel's worship rehearses the whole story of grace so far, from wandering exile to promised land, and calls Israel God's own treasured people, a pattern and a title the New Testament takes up to describe the church, gathered from every nation through Christ, as God's treasured possession.

quiz.q: How does the worshiper describe his ancestor in the firstfruits confession, verse 5?

options.text: 'My father was a wandering Aramean,' who went to Egypt few in number and became a great nation.
options.note: Verse 5 gives this exact confession.

options.text: 'My father was a great king who conquered many lands.'
options.note: The confession is one of humble beginnings, not conquest.

options.text: 'My father was born in the promised land.'
options.note: The confession specifically recalls Egypt and wandering, not the land.

options.text: No confession is recited at the firstfruits offering.
options.note: Verses 5-10 give the confession in full.

quiz.q: What does the worshiper do after reciting the confession and presenting the firstfruits basket?

options.text: Bow down before the LORD and rejoice in all the good things the LORD has given, along with the Levite and the foreigner.
options.note: Verses 10-11 describe this explicitly.

options.text: Immediately return home without any further act of worship.
options.note: The text explicitly describes bowing and rejoicing.

options.text: Burn the basket entirely as a whole offering.
options.note: The text describes the basket placed before the LORD and shared in rejoicing, not burned.

options.text: No further action is described.
options.note: Verses 10-11 describe explicit action.

quiz.q: Who receives the tithe in the third year, according to verse 12?

options.text: The Levite, the foreigner, the fatherless, and the widow.
options.note: Verse 12 names these four groups explicitly.

options.text: Only the king and his officials.
options.note: The text specifies the vulnerable, not royal officials.

options.text: Only the priests at the central sanctuary.
options.note: The text specifies a broader group including foreigners and the fatherless, not priests alone.

options.text: No recipients are named.
options.note: Verse 12 explicitly names the recipients.

quiz.q: What title does the LORD give Israel in verse 18?

options.text: His treasured possession.
options.note: Verse 18 states this explicitly.

options.text: His hired servants.
options.note: The text uses the language of treasured possession, not hired service.

options.text: His distant relatives.
options.note: The text describes a close, chosen relationship, not mere distant kinship.

options.text: No title is given.
options.note: Verse 18 gives an explicit title.

quiz.q: What does verse 19 promise will happen if Israel keeps the covenant?

options.text: The LORD will set them high in praise, name, and honor above all the nations, and they will be a holy people to Him.
options.note: Verse 19 states this explicitly.

options.text: Israel will be forgotten among the nations.
options.note: The text promises the opposite, honor above the nations.

options.text: Israel will become identical to every other nation.
options.note: The text promises distinction, not sameness.

options.text: No promise is given.
options.note: Verse 19 gives an explicit promise.

questions: Israel's worship began with an honest confession of humble, even desperate, beginnings, 'a wandering Aramean.' How honest are you in recounting your own story of need before you turn to praise?
The firstfruits offering gave God the first and best before anything else was used. Where in your life are you giving God the leftovers rather than the first and best?
The third-year tithe was directed specifically to the Levite, foreigner, fatherless, and widow. Who are the vulnerable people in your own community that your generosity should be reaching?

Israel is called God's 'treasured possession,' a title the New Testament gives to the whole church in Christ. How does knowing you are personally treasured by God change how you see your own worth today?
This chapter ties genuine worship to reciting exactly what God has done. What specific things has God done in your own life that you need to recount and give Him thanks for today?

translationNotes.note: 'A wandering Aramean' most likely refers to Jacob, whose life and family were bound up with Aram-Naharaim; the phrase emphasizes precarious, landless beginnings before God's rescue.

This third-year tithe for the vulnerable may be the same tithe described in chapter 14 redirected in its third-year cycle, or a distinct additional provision; either way it establishes a structured, recurring provision for the poor within Israel's worship calendar.
'Treasured possession' translates segullah, a term used of a king's own private treasure, first applied to Israel at Sinai in Exodus 19:5 and later applied to the church in 1 Peter 2:9.
